chunk_summary: Defines moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in Indian clinics, emphasizing diagnostic criteria, misclassification risks, and operational guidelines for outpatient management using WHO-aligned metrics like MUAC and weight-for-height z-scores.
learning_objectives: Understand the diagnostic criteria for moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in children aged 6–59 months.
Differentiate between MAM and other forms of malnutrition, such as underweight or stunting.
Learn to identify and manage uncomplicated MAM versus wasting with complications in outpatient settings.
Recognize common misclassification pitfalls and ensure accurate measurements of MUAC and oedema.
key_facts: MAM is defined as weight-for-height/length z-scores between -2 and -3 and/or MUAC 115–124 mm, with no bilateral pitting oedema.
Indian clinics often use 70–80% of median weight-for-height or MUAC 115–125 mm as operational thresholds.
Underweight (low weight-for-age) should not be equated with MAM, as it may indicate chronic stunting.
Oedema should be checked by pressing both feet for several seconds and observing for persistent pitting.
Borderline measurements should be repeated and triangulated with clinical signs like recent weight loss or poor intake.
WHO-aligned bedside screening includes visible wasting, oedema check, and MUAC or weight-for-length measurement.
reference_quote: WHO defines moderate wasting as weight-for-height/length (WHZ/WLZ) between -2 and -3 z-scores and/or MUAC 115–124 mm, with no bilateral pitting oedema. (Source: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK601642/)
document_key_topics: Diagnostic criteria for moderate acute malnutrition (MAM)
